findings.summary: The off_topic topic instructs the agent to "ask whether they want to escalate to a human agent," directly contradicting the remit's Non-Goal that the agent must never escalate to a live human and the escalation topic's own "not designed to escalate to a human" instruction.

All agent guardrails — injection resistance, grounding-only answers, no-DML, and system-prompt secrecy — exist solely as natural-language system-prompt instructions in haaHelpAgent.agent with no deterministic code-level enforcement, so a jailbreak that defeats the prompt has no independent backstop in the repo.

Retrieved Salesforce Knowledge content from the Data Cloud RAG / Agentforce Data Library (a semantic/embedding-backed store) is injected into the LLM context as grounding with no code-level provenance labeling or hidden-instruction filtering, and the system prompt only tells the agent to disregard instructions from the user (not from retrieved articles), leaving indirect prompt-injection and content-poisoning via edited articles undefended.

The shipped agent bundle defaults to citations disabled and empty RAG/citation config (citations_enabled: False, citations_url: "", rag_feature_config_id: ""), so out-of-box the agent has no bound knowledge source and no source attribution until the deployer completes Data Library binding — a default that conflicts with the remit's require-citations-when-available and grounded-answers-only intent.

No structured, durable action/audit logging exists in the repo — only debug-gated console.log and localStorage perf timing — and every remit-required log event (knowledge calls, topic routing, session lifecycle, escalation redirects) depends on platform features (Einstein Audit, Session Tracing) the README marks merely "optional but recommended," leaving the agent's behavior with no accountability trail by default.

The LWC component (haaInlineEnhancedChat.js) injects the Embedded Messaging bootstrap via a raw <script src> from admin-configured siteUrl/bootstrapScriptUrl with no trusted-domain validation, whereas the standalone static-resource JS enforces an isTrustedSalesforceUrl() https allowlist — the LWC path omits this defense-in-depth control against loading a script from an unintended origin.

The LWC input path applies no maximum-length cap on the user query before submission, while the standalone JS enforces a 1000-character MAX_QUERY_LENGTH limit — an inconsistent input-hardening posture between the two UI components.

Session identifiers and performance data are held in browser localStorage (Salesforce's {orgId}_CWC_WEB_STORAGE plus HAA_perf/sfui_perf), readable by any script sharing the DOM — a session-token exposure that becomes material in the accelerator's third-party embed mode where the widget runs alongside untrusted host-page scripts.

Origin access control for third-party embeds relies entirely on the deployer correctly configuring Embedded Service Deployment Trusted Domains and CORS; the accelerator ships no allowlist enforcement of its own, so a wildcard or misconfigured Trusted Domains entry would let an unauthorized origin host the chat widget.

The three canned-prompt custom labels (HAA_canned_prompt_*, protected: false) are submitted verbatim to the agent as pre-authored queries, so any party with org metadata-edit access can control pre-formatted inputs to the agent — a low-likelihood but real controllable-input surface flagged as a heightened-risk area in the remit.
